coredata.dc:title: How an environmental issue could turn into useful high-valued products: The olive mill wastewater case
coredata.dc:creator: Sousa, D.A.
Costa, A.I.
Alexandre, M.R.
Prata, J.V.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanomaterials have been directly synthesized from olive mill wastewaters (OMWWs) for the first time, using expedite and simple environmental-friendly procedures. The OMWWs collected from a mill operating by a two-phase centrifugation system, after being heated (150–300 °C) solely or in the presence of additives for 2–12 h, furnished nanostructured materials in high yields. Under an optimised set of reaction conditions here described, the resultant as-synthetized aqueous dispersions of carbon nanoparticles exhibit outstanding fluorescence emission properties, which encompass an astonishing quantum yield (Φ F > 0.4). The as-prepared carbon nanomaterials show excitation-dependent emissions covering the whole visible spectrum, with a predominant high glow in the blue-green region, and a remarkable photostability. The relevant features attained by the nanomaterials here reported, allied to their easy synthesis and carbon source affordability, render them with unique capabilities to be used in several current and emerging technological applications, namely in bioimaging and nanomedicine, sensorial analysis, (photo)catalysis and optoelectronics. The as-synthesized nanoparticles show a remarkable high sensitivity and selectivity towards haemoglobin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Olive mill wastewater
Waste valorisation
Carbon nanomaterials
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Haemoglobin detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of CNMs/CNDs from OMWW
Characterisation of OMWWs used as raw materials
Synthesis and structural characterisation of CNMs/C-dots from OMWW-2Ph-16
Influence of reaction parameters on CNMs/CNDs luminescence
Variability of OMWW and CNMs/CNDs luminescence

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia/Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Secretaria de Estado da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior do Maranhão

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa for funding the study (grants IPL/2016/NANOLIVE/ISEL and IPL/2017/NANOCHIRAL/ISEL ) and Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia/Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior ( UID/QUI/00616/2013 ), Portugal, for other financial support. We thank Prof. Paula Cantinho da Silva (ISEL, Portugal) for her assistance in COD/CBO 5 experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of as-synthesized CNMs/CNDs obtained from 20 µg/mL (top) and 100 µg/mL (bottom) aqueous dispersions.
UV-Vis (red line), excitation (blue line; monitored at 410 nm) and emission (green line; λexc = 340 nm) spectra of aqueous dispersions (0.1 mg/mL) of as-synthesized CNMs/CNDs.
Normalised emission spectra of aqueous dispersions (0.1 mg/mL) of as-synthesized CNMs/CNDs upon excitation in 340–420 nm range.

Emission spectra of CNMs/CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) in the presence of increasing amounts of metHgb in phosphate buffer solution at 25 ºC (pH = 7.2). Inset: Stern-Volmer plot after monitoring the change in fluorescence emission integral of each sample; λexc = 340 nm.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values expressed as percentage of TS in effluent.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Typical reaction conditions: OMWW-2Ph-16 (120 mL), ED (1.3–5.2 mmol), 250 °C, 4.0 MPa, 8 h; stirring.

head.ce:title: How an environmental issue could turn into useful high-valued products: The olive mill wastewater case

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Engenharia Química, Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa, Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa, R. Conselheiro Emídio Navarro, 1, 1959-007 Lisboa, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Química-Vila Real, Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, 5000-801 Vila Real, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Departamento de Engenharia Química, Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa, Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa, R. Conselheiro Emídio Navarro, 1, 1959-007 Lisboa, Portugal and Centro de Química-Vila Real, Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, 5000-801 Vila Real, Portugal.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Engenharia Química, Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa, Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa, R. Conselheiro Emídio Navarro, 1, 1959-007 Lisboa, Portugal
Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Hovione FarmaCiência SA, Loures, Portugal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have been directly synthesized from olive mill wastewaters (OMWWs) for the first time, using expedite and simple environmental-friendly procedures. The OMWWs collected from a mill operating by a two-phase centrifugation system, after being heated (150–300 °C) solely or in the presence of additives for 2–12 h, furnished nanostructured materials in high yields. Under an optimised set of reaction conditions here described, the resultant as-synthetized aqueous dispersions of carbon nanoparticles exhibit outstanding fluorescence emission properties, which encompass an astonishing quantum yield (Φ F > 0.4). The as-prepared carbon nanomaterials show excitation-dependent emissions covering the whole visible spectrum, with a predominant high glow in the blue-green region, and a remarkable photostability. The relevant features attained by the nanomaterials here reported, allied to their easy synthesis and carbon source affordability, render them with unique capabilities to be used in several current and emerging technological applications, namely in bioimaging and nanomedicine, sensorial analysis, (photo)catalysis and optoelectronics. The as-synthesized nanoparticles show a remarkable high sensitivity and selectivity towards haemoglobin.

ce:section.ce:para: Olive oil is obtained from the fruits of the olive tree (Olea europaea L.) through combined mechanical and physical unit operations, which may include pressing, washing, decantation, centrifugation and filtration. Olive oil has a high dietetic and nutritional value, with an average world production of 2.6 million tons/year in the last five years (International Olive Council (IOC), 2018). Olive oil production is a tremendously important agro-industrial activity in the Mediterranean basin, accounting for nearly 90% of the world quota (International Olive Council (IOC), 2018).

The olive oil is typically extracted from the olive paste obtained after crushing and malaxation of olives by two main processes: batch press (P) and continuous centrifugation. The last one operates either by a two-phase (2Ph) or a three-phase (3Ph) centrifugation process. In general, two kinds of residues are produced: i) a wet residue called pomace (press and 3Ph processes), which has found its own valorisation way through well-known techniques and end-uses (e.g. production of raffinate oils, biodiesel and soaps), and ii) an aqueous residue named olive mill wastewater (OMWW-P or OMWW-3Ph) (Tsagaraki et al., 2007). When the 2Ph centrifugation process is used instead, a pomace with much higher water content (55–70%) is produced, along with an aqueous residue (OMWW-2Ph) retrieved from the end-washings of the olive oil (Tsagaraki et al., 2007). Albeit not so easily processed in comparison to the pomace of the two other extraction systems due to the huge amount of water present, industrial strategies for oil recovery and the overall management of the wet pomace from 2Ph systems are already established and in use. Depending on the particular process used, 200–1600 L of olive mill wastewater is produced per ton of processed olives (Tsagaraki et al., 2007). The press and 2Ph processing systems generate the less amount of aqueous effluents, being the later the technological process currently more widely used (Tsagaraki et al., 2007). The OMWWs are typically characterised by their low biodegradability parameters (COD up to 220 g O2/L and BOD up to 100 g O2/L) (Paraskeva & Diamadopoulos, 2006; Hajjouji et al., 2008) which pose serious issues for its treatment. The OMWW composition is strongly dependent on the extraction process, on the type and ripening state of olives, harvest region and climate (Paraskeva & Diamadopoulos, 2006). For example, the reported amounts of phenols and polyphenols may vary between 1.3 and 4.0% (dry-weight basis) while the total carbohydrates may account for 3.4–33.0% (Paredes et al., 1999), clearly reflecting the aforementioned dependencies. The effluent toxicity/phytotoxicity and its poor biodegradability are normally attributed to the presence of high levels of phenol- and polyphenol-type compounds which are toxic to most microorganisms, imparting a great impact on land and water environments.

Processes and technologies for OMWWs treatment and, in some cases, its valorisation, have been intensively investigated over the past 70 years, having the main methodologies been reviewed (Tsagaraki et al., 2007; Paraskeva & Diamadopoulos, 2006; Valta et al., 2015; Gebreyohannes et al., 2016; Koutsos et al., 2018). These include physicochemical treatment (e.g. membrane filtration, coagulation-flocculation), aerobic and anaerobic digestion, co-digestion, combined physicochemical and biological methods and oxidation/advanced oxidation processes (e.g. ozone/H2O2, UV/H2O2, Fenton and photo-Fenton). Albeit the efforts dedicated to the study of this effluent, it still represents a serious environmental issue that claims for new strategies focused on global waste valorisation towards an effective establishment of practical, simple and sustainable industrial processes.

Within this framework, we herein describe a new approach for the rational (re)use of a natural resource which encompasses the direct conversion of several types of wastewaters resulting from industrial olive mills into carbon nanomaterials (CNMs). The CNMs thus produced are characterised by having at least one of its dimensions between 1 and 100 nm, with the smallest ones falling into the category of carbon nanodots (CNDs), featuring a (nearly)spherical morphology, dimensions <10 nm and photoluminescence (Zuo et al., 2016). Since their serendipitous discovery (Xu et al., 2004; Sun et al., 2006), a great structural diversity of carbon dots have been obtained through a large set of synthetic methods (Zuo et al., 2016; Baptista et al., 2015; Schwenke et al., 2015), carbon sources (Lim et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2007; Sahu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013), and additives and/or passivating agents.

In the last ten years, carbon dots have acquire a noteworthy place in the field of nanomaterials specially due to their outstanding luminescence, photostability and neglectable cytotoxicity, particularly in comparison with metallic quantum dots and semiconductor quantum dots (Zuo et al., 2016; Lim et al., 2015), prompting their use in the field of clinical/biological diagnosis and nanomedicine (Zuo et al., 2016; Miao et al., 2015; Luo et al., 2013). Moreover, their use have spread in recent years into the fields of chemo and biological sensors (Baptista et al., 2015), as well as (co)catalysts in photocatalytic processes (Wang et al., 2017).

As will be described, the direct conversion of OMWWs following eco-friendly ways allowed us to prepare highly fluorescent CNMs/CNDs in quite respectable yields following simple synthetic and isolation procedures. The work here described for the first time actively contributes to parenthetically reduce the environmental impact of water-based olive mill effluents. As a practical demonstration of CNMs/CNDs applicability, a direct fluorimetric detection of haemoglobin was performed.

The OMWWs used in the study were collected from an industrial olive extraction unit in Santarém region, Portugal, operating by a two-phase centrifugation process. After collection, the OMWWs were kept refrigerated at −15 °C in polyethylene bottles until use. Before each utilisation, the OMWWs were thermally stabilised at ca. 20 °C and homogenised through vigorous shaking. Two OMWW samples were retrieved: one in November 2016 (OMWW-2Ph-16) and another in November 2017 (OMWW-2Ph-17).

Gallic acid (97.5%, Sigma), tannic acid (pure, Carlo Erba), vanillin (99%, Merck), β-d-glucose monohydrate (for biochemistry and microbiology, Merck), quercetin.2H2O (crystallised, Merck), bovine serum albumin (>96%, Sigma-Aldrich), human haemoglobin (lyophilized powder; Sigma, H7379; methaemoglobin form (metHgb)), ferrous stabilised human haemoglobin A0 (lyophilized powder; Sigma, H0267; ferrous haemoglobin form (oxyHgb)), lysozyme from chicken egg white (≥90%, Aldrich, L6876), quinine hemisulphate monohydrate (>98%, Fluka) and ethylenediamine (>99.5%, Fluka) were used as received. Urea was recrystallized from ethanol. All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and were purified and/or dried by standard methods. Ultrapure water was used throughout in the preparation of aqueous solutions/dispersions.
Spectra/Por® 6 pre-wetted dialysis tubing with a MWCO of 1 kDa and a flat width of 18 mm was used for dialysis.

The OMWWs from a two-phase centrifugation process (OMWW-2Ph) were characterised for their total solids, total phenols, flavonoids, tannins, total carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, ashes, CHN content, conductivity and pH. The pristine OMWWs were diluted as necessary before the analytical procedures. Unless stated otherwise, the analytical determinations were conducted in triplicate. Total solids (TS) were determined by evaporation to dryness of OMWW samples (100 mL) followed by drying under vacuum at 105 °C until constant weight (duplicate). Total phenols were quantified by the Folin-Ciocalteu method by adapting literature procedures (Singleton & Rossi, 1965; Hajimahmoodi et al., 2013), using reference standards of gallic acid, tannic acid and vanillin in a concentration range of 75 to 250 μg/mL (additional experimental details may be found in Supplementary Material). The flavonoids content was determined by adapting a reported spectrometric method (Pekal & Pyrynska, 2014) using quercetin as reference standard in a concentration range of 5 to 75 μg/mL (additional experimental details may be found in Supplementary Material). The amount of tannins was estimated after their precipitation with hide-powder (McCandlish, 1942) followed by a colorimetric analysis of total phenols in the filtrate. Briefly, to a given sample of OMWW was added hide-powder (25 mg/mg TS) and the mixture stirred for 30 min; after filtration under vacuum, the amount of total phenols was determined in an aliquot of the filtrate, following the Folin-Ciocalteu method as above, from which the amount of tannic fraction was calculated by the difference between the amounts of total phenols in pristine OMWW and that in the filtrate. The total carbohydrates content was determined by a spectrometric procedure adapted from the literature (Dubois et al., 1956), using β-d-glucose as reference standard in a concentration range of 10–50 μg/mL (additional experimental details may be found in Supplementary Material). Proteins were determined by a reported procedure (Sedmak & Grossberg, 1977), using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as reference standard in a concentration range of 2–50 μg/mL (additional experimental details may be found in Supplementary Material). Lipids were quantified by extraction of an aliquot (250 mL) of OMWW with n-hexane (3 × 100 mL); the organic phases were dried over anhydrous magnesium sulphate, filtered, evaporated to dryness, and dried under vacuum at 25 °C. The ashes were determined by a literature procedure (Sluiter et al., 2008), using ca. 200 mg of TS from OMWW and heating at 575 °C during 24 h. COD and BOD5 were determined by reported methods (American Public Health Association, American Water Works Association and Water Environment Federation, 1997).

Infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured on a Bruker Vertex 70 or Tensor 27 using a spectral resolution of 2 cm−1 in transmission mode in KBr pellets unless stated otherwise. Band assignments were made by indicating the nature of the vibration [stretching (str), bending (ben), symmetric (sym) and asymmetric (asym)].

1H NMR (400 MHz) and decoupled 13C NMR (100 MHz) spectra were collected on a Bruker AVANCE II+ spectrometer (400 MHz) at 25 °C using solutions/dispersions of CNMs and OMWWs in D2O. The reported chemical shifts (δ/ppm) are internally referenced to residual solvent signals (1H NMR: D2O, 4.790 ppm; and 13C NMR: CD3OD, 49.000 ppm). The 1H NMR spectrum of a brand of virgin olive oil was acquired in CD2Cl2 and the chemical shifts are referenced to the signal of residual solvent (5.320 ppm). The splitting parameters for 1H NMR are denoted as follows: s (singlet) and m (multiplet).
UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Jasco J-815 CD spectrometer at 20 °C using 1-cm quartz cells; unless noted otherwise, aqueous solutions/dispersions were used in the determinations.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer LS45 fluorimeter using a 1-cm quartz cuvette in right angle (RA) geometry at 25 °C. Fluorescence quantum yields (Φ F) were measured in aqueous solutions using quinine sulphate in 0.01 M H2SO4 (Φ F = 0.54; air-equilibrated conditions, RA geometry) as reference standard at 25 °C. To prevent inner filter effects during quantum yield measurements the optical density (OD) of the samples and reference were kept below 0.05 at the excitation wavelength. The quantum yields were calculated by the slope method, according to a reported procedure (Annon, 2018). Fluorescence (emission and excitation) spectra were recorded with the same operating settings.

Elemental analyses (CHNS) were determined in duplicate in a Carlo Erba EA 1108 analyser, being the combustion performed at 1015 °C during 900 s with an oxygen flux of 15 mL/min, at C.A.C.T.I., Universidad de Vigo, Spain.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out in an Hitachi H-8100 microscope (lantanium hexaboride filament, 200 kV), with a point-to-point resolution of 2.7 Å using a 200 mesh copper grid covered with a Formvar Carbon film, at MicroLab, Instituto Superior Técnico, Lisbon. The CNM water dispersions were deposited in the support and left to evaporate naturally at ca. 20 °C.
The pH of solutions/dispersions were determined at ca. 25 °C with a pH Denver Instrument 215 or with a WTW-Multi 340i meter equipped with a pH electrode SENTIX 41-3.
A WTW-Multi 340i conductivity meter equipped with a WTW Tetra Com® 325 electrode was used for conductivimetry analysis, being the temperature and concentration of the samples given for each assay.

Biosensing assays were performed with aqueous dispersions of CNMs/CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) obtained under the conditions reported in Table 3, entry 3. For titration experiments, the test solutions were prepared in aqueous buffered solutions (50 mM phosphate buffer, pH = 7.2) at various CNMs/CNDs:Hgb ratios by varying the Hgb concentration from 10.0 nM to 2.0 μM in separated volumetric flasks. The air-equilibrated solutions were kept at 25 °C for 10 min prior to spectrofluorimetric determination. After exciting at the desire wavelength (340 nm, unless stated otherwise), the area under the fluorescence emission curves were taken to derive the sensitivity of the nanoparticles towards Hgb making use of the Stern-Volmer equation F 0/F = 1 + K SV [Q], where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CNDs in the absence and presence of the quencher, [Q] is the quencher concentration and K SV is the static Stern–Volmer constant (Lakowicz, 2006).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of CNMs/CNDs from OMWW
ce:section.ce:para: The OMWW-2Ph-16 (120 mL), containing a TS content of 2.61 g/L and a pH of 4.7, was fed into a high-pressure reactor (Parr, model 4560; 300 mL of capacity) equipped with pressure, temperature and stirring sensors/controllers (Parr, model 4843), with or without additives, under air-equilibrated conditions. The contents of the reactor were gradually heated until the desired temperature was reached and kept at that temperature for a certain time. After the thermochemical conversion, the reaction mixture was left to cool to room temperature (ca. 20–25 °C) and filtered through a 0.20 μm cellulose membrane, resulting in a membrane residue and an aqueous dispersion containing the CNMs/CNDs.

For quantification purposes, an aliquot (20 mL) of the aqueous dispersion so obtained (ca. 120 mL) was taken to dryness and dried under vacuum at 105 °C. The insoluble residue was quantified by direct weighing.

The structural characterisation of the as-prepared CNMs/CNDs was accomplished by FTIR, NMR and elemental analysis using solid samples, while for UV–Vis, fluorescence and COD analysis the aqueous dispersions were directly used after appropriate dilution. The TEM analysis was also performed from the as-synthesized aqueous dispersions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of OMWWs used as raw materials
ce:section.ce:para: The main physico-chemical characteristics of OMWWs used in the study are displayed in Table 1 .
The proximate analysis for their organic constituents furnished the data gathered in Table 2 .
Since most of the study was carried out with OMWW-2Ph-16, the following structural characterisation pertains to it.

The pristine OMWWs did not show any relevant luminescence, as attested by the calculated quantum yield (0.0061). The infrared analysis revealed the spectral profile shown in Fig. S1, with characteristic bands appearing at 3414 (OH, str), 3240 (NH, str), 3007 (CH olefinics, str), 2955 (CH3, str asym), 2925 (CH2, str asym), 2872 (CH3, str sym), 2855 (CH2, str sym), 1746 (CO ester, str), 1644 (CO, str acids/amides), 1567 (NH ben/CC str), 1460 (CH2, ben), 1380 (CH3, ben), 1240, 1163, 1120, 1095 and 1035 (C(O)OC, str and CO, str) cm−1. The 1H NMR (Fig. S2A) presents signals of a lipidic fraction with multiplets centred at 0.91 (CH3 acyclic aliphatic chains), 1.33 ((CH2)n acyclic chains), 1.61 (CH 2CH2CO2R), 2.05 (CH 2 CHCH, acyclics), 2.27 (CH2CO2R), 4.12 and 4.31 (CH2O-acyl, glycerol), 5.25 and 5.34 (CH CH, unsaturated fatty acids) and 6.2–8.5 (ArH) ppm, the last showing weak intensities. Additionally, the resonances due to saccharides/polysaccharides appear in the range 3.25–4.25 ppm. For comparison, the 1H NMR spectrum of the olive oil obtained under the same extraction system and mill is shown in Fig. S2B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterisation of CNMs/C-dots from OMWW-2Ph-16
ce:section.ce:para: The new luminescent carbon nanomaterials here described may be directly synthesized from OMWWs in an eco-friendly straightforward manner. Depending on the particular origin and nature of the OMWWs, the concentration of total solids to be processed may be adjusted by dilution with water or with aqueous effluents originated from other more diluted extraction methods in order to achieve the desired concentration for the conversion. Usually, TS concentrations between 2.5 g/L and 60 g/L may be used.

Following the general procedure and using ethylenediamine (ED) as additive (1.3 mmol/g TS in OMWW) and a dwell time of 8 h at 250 °C, an aqueous amber dispersion of CNMs/CNDs and a black residue corresponding to 7.5% (w/w, based on TS and ED) were obtained after membrane filtration. An aqueous re-dispersion of the above black material showed that it is essentially non-fluorescent. Its FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3) shows the most prominent bands at 3420, 2923, 2851, 1703, 1618 and 1458 cm−1. Under the foregoing reaction conditions, the yield of the aqueous dispersed CNMs/CNDs was 39.7% (w/w, based on TS and ED). FTIR analysis (Fig. 1 ) showed characteristic bands at 3420, 3245, 2963, 2932, 2858, 1655, 1568, 1410, 652 and 618 cm−1. In comparison to OMWW-2Ph-16, it can be observed that the OH and NH (3650–3100 cm−1) bands increase in intensity, while those corresponding to the aliphatic components (3030–2800 cm−1, CH) present a sharp decrease, along with the disappearance of the ester band (1746 cm−1) and the concomitant appearance of new bands at 1655, 1568 cm−1and 1410 cm−1 assignable to acid/carboxylate, amide I and II, CN, and CC vibrations.

The NMR spectroscopy was used to get a glimpse of the main type of functionalities and/or carbon typologies at the nanoparticles surface. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S4) of the as-synthesized CNMs/CNDs shows a set of signals corresponding to aliphatic C H between 0.8 and 2.5 ppm, while those appearing at 3.25–4.25 ppm could be assigned to CH N and CH O, and between 6.5 and 8.6 ppm from resonances belonging to arylic hydrogens. The signals observed in 13C NMR (Fig. S5) are of weak intensity. Nonetheless, three regions can be distinguished: 20–40 ppm, corresponding to aliphatic CH, 50–75 ppm from C-sp3 present in CHN and CHO moieties, and resonances near 120–145 ppm and 180 ppm that may be assigned, respectively, to C-sp2 from arylic and olefinic carbons, and quaternary carbons from carboxylic/carboxylate groups, phenols/phenolates, quinoid structures and amides.

The microanalysis of this material furnished the following composition after correction for ashes (28.5%): C, 54.76%; H, 6.68%; N, 4.20% and O (calculated), 34.37%. By comparison with the pristine OMWW-2Ph-16, the N/C molar ratio is increased from 0.022 to 0.066, and that of O/C from 0.26 to 0.47, meaning that both the nitrogen and oxygen contents in the as-prepared CNMs/CNDs have been raised significantly. The H/C ratio decreased but to a lesser extent (1.58 to 1.45). The O/N ratio was also lowered (11.7 to 7.2).

Analysis of the ash contents by FTIR indicated the presence of carbonates (very strong broad stretching vibration nearly 1420 cm−1) as the main component (Miller & Wilkins, 1952). Its origin may be traced to the mineral fraction already present in the original effluent and trapped carbon dioxide evolved from organic carbon degradation during the thermochemical conversion (Fig. S6).

It should be mentioned that our attempts to purify the bare CNMs/CNDs by dialysis, namely for removing inorganic components, did not work. Indeed, when a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1 kDa was used (24 h dialysing against ultrapure water), most of the luminescent CNDs permeate the membrane. These showed a slight improvement in quantum yield whereas the material that remained inside the bag represents <10% of the initial feed and is poorly fluorescent. This behaviour is not unprecedented (Song et al., 2014) but, amazingly enough, the method is one the most widely reported for purification of carbon dots.
The conductivity of aqueous dispersions (0.1 mg/mL) of CNMs/CNDs (average of two batches) was found to be 45.5 μS/cm at 25 °C.

TEM analysis was used to morphologically characterise the synthesized CNMs. It was found that aqueous dispersions of nanoparticles at a concentration of 20 μg/mL led to the formation of agglomerates comprehending very small spherical nanoparticles with dimensions <2.5 nm (Fig. 2A). The presence of larger nanoparticles, or their aggregates/agglomerates, with diameters up to ca. 75 nm was also observed (Fig. 2B and Fig. S7), particularly when more concentrated dispersions of CNMs (100 μg/mL) were used in the deposition.

The ground-state absorption and steady-state fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra of aqueous dispersions of CNMs/CNDs obtained as above are shown in Fig. 3 . The UV–Vis is characterised by well-defined bands peaking at ca. 239, 273 and 320 nm, being the bands at higher energy probably traceable to π-π* transitions of carbogeneous core (C-sp2 network) and that at higher wavelengths to n-π* transitions or mixed n-π* and π-π* transitions assignable to functionalities (e.g. CO, amides/acids, CN) covalently linked to the surface of the carbon matrix. When excited at 340 nm, a blue emission results (Fig. 3) with a λ em max centred at 407 nm, with a Stokes shift of 0.85 eV (90 nm) and a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 0.54 eV (74 nm). The excitation spectrum (monitored at 410 nm) indicates contributions to the emission from the chromophores with transitions at 242 and 336 nm, being the later clearly predominant (Fig. 3).

The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ F) of the as-synthesized CNMs/CNDs reached 0.42 when excited at 340 nm and 0.20 with 380 nm excitation. This result is in line with other reaction batches using the same conditions and raw materials affording in general Φ F in the order of 0.35 ± 0.05. These values revealed that the CNMs/CNDs obtained from OMWWs are highly fluorescent, surely prompting their use in a lot of potential applications where luminescence should be a must.

It was also found that the luminescence of the as-prepared materials could be improved on treating the aforementioned aqueous dispersions with an organic extractant (e.g. dichloromethane and/or ethyl acetate); the recovered (>95%) aqueous dispersions of nanomaterials display an enhanced quantum yield which may exceed over 40% of the initial value. Conversely, the extracted organic phase is virtually non-emissive and mainly consisted of lipid-derived components as inferred from FTIR analysis (film; strong bands at 2956, 2923–2918, 2872 (sh), 2852, 1734–1713 and 1650 cm−1; Fig. S8).

The pH effect on CNMs/CNDs luminescence was next evaluated by varying the pH of aqueous dispersions between 1 and 12 through the addition of aqueous solutions of HCl or NaOH to an as-prepared aqueous dispersion having an initial pH of 5.63, while keeping the concentration of nanoparticles at 0.1 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. S9A, upon illumination of the sample at 340 nm, the fluorescence emission (λ exc max = 407 nm) is kept nearly constant over the pH range of 1.0 through 7.5, decreasing sharply afterwards until reaching ca. 10% of its initial intensity at pH 12.3. The profiles of the emission bands do not show any visible change until pH = 11.0 (Fig. S9B); at pH 11.7 and 12.7, the emission maxima suffered slight bathochromic shifts (5 and 14 nm, respectively). Noteworthy, the synthesized materials are pH responsive. Indeed, when the pH is reverted from 11.0 to 7.3 or from 11.7 to 1.4, the emission intensities are recovered by at least 85% of their initial values. This behaviour is consistent with the establishment of successive acid/base equilibria during the change of pH which would chiefly involve the acidic functionalities at the CNMs/CNDs surfaces.

The photostability of aqueous dispersions of CNMs/CNDs at 0.1 mg/mL (pH = 5.63) was checked under continuous ultraviolet radiation (λ exc = 340 nm). The experiment showed that after up to 5 h of continuous radiation, the loss of fluorescence intensity (monitored at 407 nm) is null (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of reaction parameters on CNMs/CNDs luminescence
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reaction temperature on the CNMs/CNDs luminescence and mass yields was first analysed following the general synthetic procedure, keeping constant the dwell time (8 h) and the amount of additive (ED; 1.3 mmol/g TS in OMWW). The results are gathered in Table 3 .

It is clear that under the tested conditions, a reaction temperature of 250 °C affords the highest luminescent nanomaterials, while maintaining a high mass yield. The progressive decrease in mass yield is consistent with the higher severity of reaction conditions which may led to an increase in gaseous products (mainly CO2, and minor amounts of CO, H2 CH4 and light CmHn) (Funke & Ziegler, 2010). From the UV–Vis (Fig. S11) and fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra (Fig. S12), it may be concluded that photophysical properties of the nanoparticles have a strong dependency on reaction temperature. The experiment conducted at 250 °C led to the highest intense absorption at ca. 320 nm and concomitantly to an excitation band peaking at around 330 nm that is the main responsible chromophore for the observed emission. The nearly symmetrical excitation and emission profiles, along with the lowest FWHM, probably mean that the CNMs/CNDs formed under these conditions are more structurally uniform.
The influence of residence time on CNMs/CNDs luminescence was next evaluated, being the results present in Table 4 .

Under the tested conditions, residence times between 6 and 8 h furnished the highest emissive nanomaterials, along with good mass yields. The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of all samples presented similar profiles; in the case of entry 5 (12 h), the absorption bands at ca. 275 and 320 nm were less defined and intense (not shown).

As is known from literature (Baptista et al., 2015), the nature of the additive/passivating agent may have a significant role on the emissive properties of CNDs. Of course this singular parameter is not unrelated to the nature of the carbon sources used. Thus, assessment of what kind of additives perform better in conjunction with the current raw material is an important task. In the small survey here presented, ethylenediamine and urea were selected as organic additives. Additionally, experiments were also conducted in the absence of any additive and with the addition of sodium hydroxide. The findings are presented in Table 5 .

Several comments are in order. First, the conversion in the absence of any additive led to nanomaterials with an appreciable quantum yield (Φ F = 0.10). On the other hand, the use of carbamide or NaOH results in poorly emissive materials, while ethylenediamine afforded the better outcome (Φ F = 0.42).

Having the aforementioned information at hand, additional experiments were also conducted with increasing amounts of the diamine (Table 6 ). From the results it can be concluded that the lowest ratio of ED/OMWW produces the highest luminescent carbon nanomaterials.

Analysis of UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S13) revealed similar profiles for entries 1 and 2, showing however an intensity increase in transitions at the low energy end (ca. above 360 nm) when the amount of ED was doubled. Further increase in ED amount (entry 3) led to the appearance of a new band around 410 nm and a decrease in intensity of the band near 320 nm. In accordance with that, the excitation spectra (Fig. S14) corresponding to entry 3 indicated the presence of new emissive entities excitable at lower energies (around 400 nm) which are responsible for the observed redshifts in the emission. For comparison, the excitation spectra of entry 1 sample monitored at various emission wavelengths are also displayed. The structural changes that occurred can also be followed by FTIR analysis. As is apparent from Fig. S15, the amide I band (1658–1652 cm−1) progressively raises its intensity on increasing the amount of ED added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variability of OMWW and CNMs/CNDs luminescence
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned early (cf. Introduction), and beyond the different extraction systems that can be used for olive oil production, the OMWWs composition may be highly variable owing, for example, to other less manageable natural causes such as the olives ripening state at the harvesting date which is in turn related to particular acclimate conditions during the growing season, and process variables. Thus, to have a wide perspective of the real possibilities of smoothly and efficiently transform a problematic aqueous effluent into value-added products such as those here described, we decide to investigate how the variability of OMWW composition would affect and impact on the synthesized CNMs/CNDs. For that, an OMWW collected at the same two-stage centrifugation mill but at another harvesting season (November 2017) was used as raw material (OMWW-2Ph-17). As can be seen from the proximate composition analysis (Table 2), significant differences exist between several of the main typified organic components, in particular a higher amount of saccharides/polysaccharides, proteins and lipids are noted. Additionally, the OMWW-2Ph-17, besides presenting a much higher TS loading (30.72 g/L), showed COD and BOD5 levels (112.5 g O2/L and 42.5 g/L, respectively; biodegradability parameter = 0.38) which are orders of magnitude higher than the previous studied effluent sample.

At first we applied the best reaction conditions settled for the OMWW-2Ph-16 to the new raw material; however, a quite disappointing fluorescence quantum yield (Φ F = 0.08) was obtained for the isolated nanoparticles dispersion. After systematically surveying a series of reaction parameters, which have included temperature, dwell time, amount of additive, changes in pH reaction medium, conversion after previous acidic hydrolysis and two-step reaction sequences, we were pleased to find that when the conversion was performed under a nitrogen atmosphere (ca. 200 kPa), after first degassing and purging the reactor contents with N2, a rewarding quantum yield of 0.3 (λ exc = 360 nm) could be obtained, even at less harsh conditions (e.g. 200 °C and 4 h of run time). Under the new developed conditions (inert atmosphere), the use of ED (e.g. 1.3 mmol/g TS) is still beneficial to attain a higher level of luminescence, however, higher ratios, for example a fourfold ratio increase, led to a quite low quantum yield (Φ F = 0.05). Moreover, rising the reaction temperature to 250 °C also produced less emissive nanoparticles (Φ F = 0.10–0.12); reduced (2 h) and prolonged (12 h) residence times do not favour the emissive properties (Φ F around 0.2 in both instances) of CNMs.

It is noteworthy to mention that a previous report dealing with the synthesis of CNDs from glycerol (Lai et al., 2012) did not observe the formation of carbon dots under inert conditions (N2), inferring that atmospheric oxygen played a key role on the reaction.
Our studies also found that the thermochemical conversion of OMWW-2Ph-17 into CNMs/CNDs by the aforesaid procedure is accompanied by a significant reduction (ca. 75%) of the initial COD values.

The UV–Vis and fluorescence data relating to CNMs/CNDs obtained from OMWW-2Ph-17 are displayed in Fig. S16. The absorption maximum presents a redshift of 15 nm, peaking now at 335 nm. As a corollary, the main excitation band peaks at 354 nm when monitored at the wavelength of maximum emission (444 nm). The most relevant bands in infrared appear at 3420, 1650 and 1595 cm−1 (Fig. S17).

The foregoing results clearly showed that for the successful conversion of OMWWs into highly luminescent carbon nanomaterials in high yields, a careful selection of reaction parameters should take place well before any attempted large scale production of these materials, owing to the variability of the OMWW batches.

As a demonstration of the usefulness of the as-prepared CNMs/CNDs, a fluorimetric biosensing assay was carried out. Haemoglobin (Hgb), a four iron-haem containing protein, was selected as the target analyte, given its crucial role in oxygen transport in living organisms, where its deficiency or abnormalities are linked to many medical conditions (e.g. leukaemia, anaemia, sickle cell anaemia, polycythaemia). Therefore accurate and practical methods for estimation of low Hgb levels in blood and other fluids are in high demand.

The interaction of CNMs/CNDs with target proteins was followed by spectrofluorimetric titration in buffered aqueous medium (pH = 7.2). Upon treatment of CNMs/CNDs aqueous dispersions (0.01 mg/mL) with increasing amounts of ferric Hgb (metHgb; Soret band peaking at 406 nm) under 340 nm illumination, a huge decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 5 ). The reduction in fluorescence emission intensity was quantified by the static Stern–Volmer constant (K SV) derived from the Stern–Volmer equation (cf. Section 2.3 for details), which is a direct measure of the sensor sensitivity.

Following that approach, a K SV of 7.3 × 105 M−1 was obtained for the full concentration range (10 nM–2 μM) of metHgb. When ferrous Hgb (oxyHgb; Soret band peaking at 414 nm) was used instead (Fig. S18), a lower response was noted (K SV = 3.6 × 105 M−1). Since the fluorescence emission spectrum of CNMs/CNDs strongly overlaps with the absorption spectra of both Hgb forms (Fig. S19), with overlap integrals (J(λ)) of 3.929 × 1015 M−1 cm−1 nm4 (metHgb) and 3.142 × 1015 M−1 cm−1 nm4 (oxyHgb) (Preus, 2018), it is conceivable that a Förster-type resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism (Lakowicz, 2006) is operating and led to CNDs emission decrease. Assuming that all other parameters involved in this non-radiative energy transfer process are identical, namely the relative orientation (κ2) of the donor (CNDs) and acceptor (Hgb) transition dipoles in the two Hgb forms, the magnitude of J(λ) would largely determine the efficiency of energy transfer and therefore the apparent K SV. Besides, in addition to FRET, a radiative heterotransfer leading to a decrease of CNDs fluorescence intensity in the region of spectral overlap due to absorption of emitted photons by the proteins may also occurs and hence contribute to the magnitude of K SV. Indeed, as the concentration of protein (metHgb) is increased throughout the titration experiment, a change in the profile of the emission bands (peaking initially at ca. 407 nm) may be witnessed (Fig. 5), clearly indicating the presence of secondary inner filter effects (IFEs) (Valeur, 2002). As the metHgb-CNDs pair has a higher spectral overlap, a larger distortion is observed in the emission spectra in comparison to oxyHgb-CNDs counterpart (Fig. S18).

It was also found that a lower K SV (4.3 × 105 M−1) is reached when the CNDs are excited at 380 nm in the presence of metHgb (Fig. S20). This result is in line with the reduction (ca. 20%) of overlap integral, since at this excitation wavelength a redshift of CNDs emission occurs (λ em max = 437 nm), and also to a decreased contribution of IFE component owing to the smaller proportion of emitted light that is absorbed by metHgb. A similar trend was observed for oxyHgb where a K SV of 2.3 × 105 M−1 was obtained upon 380 nm excitation.

Previous reports on Hgb detection using carbon dots prepared from several carbon sources and synthetic methods (Huang et al., 2015; Bui & Park, 2016; Barati et al., 2015) had also showed a high level of detection, with K SV varying between ca. 2.7–3.3 × 105 M−1, either with metHgb (Huang et al., 2015; Bui & Park, 2016) or oxyHgb (Barati et al., 2015).

In order to discard the possibility that the observed decrease in emission intensity was due to nonspecific interactions established between CNDs and proteins, leading to aggregation/agglomeration of nanoparticles and concomitant self-quenching (Fan et al., 2002), a control experiment with the highly basic protein lysozyme (Lys), devoid of any prosthetic groups, was performed. As expected, no fluorescence quenching was observed under otherwise identical experimental conditions (Fig. S21).
The above findings revealed a noteworthy sensing ability and selectivity of our CNMs/CNDs towards Hgb.

Our findings show that wastewaters from olive mill processing units behave as perfect carbon sources (and that of other elements) for the synthesis of highly luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials. Through simple one-step eco-friendly thermochemical methodologies and minimal post-processing operations, we have shown that OMWWs can be directly converted into CNMs/CNDs in high yields using easily scalable-up processes. By a systematic search of the best conditions for the transformation of a troublesome aqueous effluent into highly luminescent nanoparticles, we were able to identify some critical reaction parameters, such as the temperature, the residence time, and the nature/amount of additive, to carry out the reaction.
Overall, our proposal seems to be a valid sustainable contribution to help mitigate the current environmental impacts associated with aqueous-based olive mill effluents.
The new luminescent CNMs/CNDs are capable of efficiently detect haem proteins (Hgb) at biological pH while clearly discriminating non-haem proteins (Lys).

The authors thank Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa for funding the study (grants IPL/2016/NANOLIVE/ISEL and IPL/2017/NANOCHIRAL/ISEL) and Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia/Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior (UID/QUI/00616/2013), Portugal, for other financial support. We thank Prof. Paula Cantinho da Silva (ISEL, Portugal) for her assistance in COD/CBO5 experiments.

J. V. P. conceived, designed and supervised the study, and wrote the manuscript. D. A. S. carried out most of the synthetic work and characterisation analysis. M. R. A. performed preliminary synthetic experiments and part of earlier OMWW characterisation. A. I. C. accompanied the management and supervision of lab work and carried out the NMR analysis. All authors analysed the data, discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.
J. V. P. and A. I. C. are inventors on a Portuguese patent application n° 110131 (filed in June 8, 2017) submitted by ISEL that covers part of the studies described in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary Material for How an Environmental Issue Could Turn Into Useful High-valued Products: The Olive Mill Wastewater Case.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. S1–S21, and additional experimental details regarding OMWW characterisation. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.08.060.

sb:author.ce:surname: American Public Health Association, American Water Works Association and Water Environment Federation
